front: Concerto: Bach's set of six concertos dedicated to Margrave Christian Ludwig — compiled as a failed job application and also called 'Six Concerts à plusieurs instruments'
Concerto: first period-instrument recording of this set was made by Nikolaus Harnoncourt in 1964
Concerto: Wendy Carlos realised this set on the Moog synthesizer in a 1979 double-album (part of the Switched-On Bach series)
Concerto: first movement features an unprecedented ~65-measure solo harpsichord cadenza marked 'senza stromenti'
Concerto: concertino consists of flute, violin, and harpsichord; in D major; first known instance of Bach using the transverse flute
Concerto: may have been composed as a showpiece for a harpsichord delivered by Michael Mietke to Bach's court at Köthen; possibly connected to a proposed duel with Louis Marchand
Concerto: second movement consists of just one bar — two half-note chords forming a Phrygian half-cadence — typically replaced or improvised over in performance
Concerto: scored for three string trios (three violins, three violas, three cellos); in G major; opens with the violin pickup motif G–F-sharp–G
Concerto: features 'flauti d'echo' (echo flute) parts, universally performed on alto recorders in modern practice; in G major
Concerto: solo violin plays the bass line when the ripieno is unaccompanied in the E-minor andante; fugal presto finale preceded by a Phrygian cadence
Concerto: scored without violins — uses violas, viola da gamba, violone, cello, and harpsichord; gamba players have an unusual supporting role
Concerto: third movement gigue is a set of variations on the ritornello theme from the first movement; scored without violins
Concerto: only concerto in the set with four movements; final movement consists of a polacca with two trios and a menuet; in F major
Concerto: features horn calls and is related to Bach's Hunting Cantata; first movement has no tempo indication and opens in cut-time with an eighth-beat pickup
Concerto: scholars debate whether its 'tromba' part was intended for trumpet or horn; both Johann Altenburg and Johann Ludwig Schreiber have been proposed as the intended soloist
